title: SLIM-Agent Pointer Memory Entry
description: A single index entry that points to external knowledge (URL) rather than storing full content. The schema is intentionally cross-platform: any tool/agent that wants to interoperate with SLIM-Agent can read/write this format. See SKILL.md 'Cross-Platform Guide' and README.md 'Roadmap - Cross-Platform' for usage context.

id.description: Globally unique identifier. Recommendation: UUID v4 or 'slim-<sha256[:12]>'.

summary.description: One-sentence conclusion or insight. The pointer's whole point: short, atomic, retrievable.

tags.description: Topic tags for fast filtering (no LLM needed).

urls.description: Where the full content lives. First URL is primary, rest are mirrors/backups. URL fetcher will use the first reachable.

source.description: How this entry was created. Useful for trust ranking.

fetched_at.description: Last successful URL fetch. NULL if never fetched.

confidence.description: Self-reported reliability [0.0, 1.0]. 1.0 = verified by user, 0.5 = auto-extracted, 0.0 = unverified.

metadata.description: Free-form additional context. Implementation-specific. Cross-platform readers MUST ignore unknown keys.

examples.summary: Pre-compaction memory flush: only write to memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md, treat SOUL/MEMORY/AGENTS/TOOLS/DREAMS as read-only.

cross_platform_notes.design_intent: This schema is the cross-platform contract. Any agent, app, or service that wants to interoperate with SLIM-Agent pointer memory can read/write this JSON format. Full content stays at the URL, not in the index.
cross_platform_notes.what_this_is_NOT: This is NOT a data sync protocol. It is a data FORMAT. Sync/transport is intentionally out of scope — see README 'Roadmap - Cross-Platform'.
future_compatibility: v0.x: Single-machine, local SQLite storage. JSON export uses this schema.
v1.x: If a desktop-class app emerges that wants cross-device sync, it can read this schema and reimplement storage/transport without losing user data.
v2.x: Cloud sync is intentionally deferred — requires a product/UX layer (auth, devices, conflicts) that no Python skill can provide.
interop_examples: A Raycast-style launcher: index user's local pointer_memory.json, show as quick-search entries.
A Notion plugin: import user's SLIM index as a Notion database, keeping URLs as page links.
Another agent (LangChain, AutoGen): read pointer_memory entries as semantic search corpus — summaries + tags, not raw content.
A future 'Personal AI' OS: if built, the OS-level memory service can adopt this schema as its native format.
